Flexibility connection agreementElectricity System Services Market
Library

Tariff design · review dimension

€

Network cost driver

What physical and economic network cost should the tariff represent?

Understand the dimensionComment on this dimension

Clear definition

What this dimension means

The network cost driver is the physical or economic reason why a tariff signal is introduced. It may be a coincident local peak, transformer capacity, voltage stress, losses, reinforcement expenditure, or another measurable driver of network cost.

If the tariff is not linked to the actual network need, customers may change behaviour without producing useful system value. A strong design therefore begins with the network problem and only then selects the charging variable.

Design choices

Choices that must be made explicitly

A reviewer should be able to identify which option is selected, why it is selected, who decides it, and how it is implemented.

Physical driver

Peak loading, congestion, voltage, losses, hosting capacity, or reinforcement need.

Charging variable

Energy, measured peak, contracted capacity, physical connection capacity, or a combination.

Cost horizon

Historical cost recovery, forward-looking incremental cost, or both.

Allocation rule

How costs are distributed across voltage levels, customer groups, locations, and periods.

Practical example

How the dimension appears in practice

A feeder experiences a winter evening peak caused by heat pumps and EV charging. An annual volumetric tariff is unlikely to address this need. A capacity or time-differentiated charge may be more relevant, but only if the signal is visible and customers can respond during the critical hours.

Review principle

The mechanism is credible only when the selected design can be explained in plain language and linked to a verifiable network, regulatory, commercial, or customer outcome.

Stakeholder implications

The same design choice creates different responsibilities and risks

DSO

Demonstrate that the selected driver corresponds to a real network cost or constraint.

Regulator

Verify cost-reflectivity, non-discrimination, and consistency with allowed revenue.

Aggregator or retailer

Understand how the cost driver affects portfolios, baselines, and retail offers.

Customer or FSP

Understand which action changes the charge and whether that action is feasible.

Questions for reviewers

What remains open?

  • Is the driver measurable at the network level where the tariff is applied?
  • Does the charging variable represent causation or only correlation?
  • Could the same cost be recovered elsewhere?
  • Which customers can actually influence the driver?
  • How often should the driver and parameter values be reviewed?

Evidence expected

What should support the decision?

  • Network loading and voltage studies
  • Forward-looking reinforcement-cost analysis
  • Coincidence and contribution analysis
  • Sensitivity to alternative charging variables
  • Distributional impact by customer group

Feedback on network cost driver

What should be clarified, added, removed, or redesigned?

Please comment specifically on the definition, design choices, stakeholder responsibilities, implementation feasibility, evidence requirements, and interaction with the other flexibility acquisition mechanisms.

Submit structured feedback
